results.title: Image 1 of Rutland weekly herald (Rutland, Vt.), April 2, 1863
description: RUTLAND HERALD V THURSDAY APRIL 2 18G3 Tbe Conscription law nod Poor Men A certain class of newspapers which occa sionally utter a loud call upon every man of every party to obey the laws whatever they be as if any but their own followers ever pro posed resistance to them are engaged at the saule time and all the time in misrepresenting the most important and necessary ones called ovietanfA hv the national struggle for IUIU j life and in fomenting disaffection thereto What can be the object of this constant labor except to stir up the very resistance to the laws which they dare not openly advocate Such is apparently enough its only tendency The conscription act of the last Congress is a law of the land If these journals are sincere in their call upon all to obey the laws why constantly misrepresent the object and effect of its provisions 1 Conscript laws of some kind have been a necessity in every great war Butprobably no law of the kind was tver framed which was so just and humane in its treatment of all classes bf the people or which so well protected the poor citizen We

description: ATUttDAYAlGt8T 1838 MR PRENTISS and the SUBTREASURY Several of the friends of Mr Prentiss have expressed their surprise that he should vote for the SubTreasury bill while some locofocos seem to be tickled most to death at the idea We did not observe the mistake but several friends have informed us that in the list of yeas and nays published in the Natchez Cou rier of the 14th July tho name of S S Prentiss is recorded in favor of the bill It is entirely a mistake Both of our rep resentatives voted against the bill John II Prentiss of New York voted for the bill but Sergeant S Prentiss dont belong to the Van Burcnitical crew that profess one thing and practice another Ad no very extensive evil is likely to result fiom the mistake it is probably all for the bejr to all parties We certainly do not ettjry our loco foco neighbors their transient de light when we reflect that all their pleas ures in the political world are now made up ot the imaginary fruition of things that are not s SteamBoat Accidents The following enumeration of the loss of life by the principal Steam Boat

results.title: Image 2 of Daily national intelligencer (Washington City [D.C.]), June 22, 1832
description: CHARLESTON UNION MEETING Charleston S C June 14 Agreeably to public notice a very large num ber of the UNION AND STATE RIGHTS PARTY assembed at Seyles on Tuesday eve ning The Hon Henry Middleton was called to the chair On assuming the officeMr Middle ton delivered the following address Gentlemen The unexpectedness of the call which has procured for me the honor of presiding in so numerous and respectable a meeting of my fellowcitizens as is here assembled and the circumstances that most of those who hear me are familiar with the history of the causes which have produced and kept up a degree of political excite ment unexampled in our State wilt prevent the possibil ity and the necessity of my entering into any details on the present occasion I shall therefore only ask your indulgent attention for a few moments After a successful progress of more than forty years under a system of Jaws where power is wisely distributed in due proportion between our National and State au thorities we see in our State the elements of strife at length gaining the ascendancy over those of order and about to destroy the equipoise hitherto maintainedthreat ening to

results.title: Image 3 of Daily national intelligencer (Washington City [D.C.]), April 29, 1847
description: WASHINGTON Liberty and Union now and forever one and inseparable THURSDAY APRIL 29 1847 Mn DALLASS LETTER Our readers will we hope perfectly understand hat in transferring to our columns from the Unionin which paper it first attracted our at entionthe Letter of Mr Dallas on the Isthmus question we do not adopt any part or particle of the Hon Vice Presidents apparent predilection for War either in the abstract or in the actual as it now exists between this country and the neighbor ing Republic We dissent from his opinion par ticularly as to the value of military glory con sidering the passion for it as one of the most fatal political diseases with which a Government or Peopleand especially a Republican Government and Peoplecan be infected Nor must we by our silence on the point subject ourselves to the I imputation of tacitly assenting that the vindication I of the National honor required this war or that the tenderest regard for that honor can or could have suggested to any wellorganized mind a neces sity for the war Nor do we conceive the acquisition of present power either for party purposes at home or for the purposes of

results.title: Image 3 of Daily national intelligencer (Washington City [D.C.]), November 15, 1850
description: NATIONAL INTELLIGENCER FROM OUR PARIS CORRESPONDENT Paris October 25 1850 The French press has been almost exclusively occupied with strange rumors of wars reaching us from England Tie information obtained through the British press is generally believed here to be ex aggerated but enough is presumed to be true to af ford reasonable apprehension of alarm for the peace of Europe I allude to the proposition said to have been made by the Governments of France and Russia to that of England for an armed interference to oppose Prussian influence in the war now waging between Denmark and the Duchies of Schleswig and Holstein Prussia was to be summoned by a joint note of the three Powers to discontinue the secret support which she has not ceased to extend to the Duchies in despite of her formal engagement to maintain a strict neutrality In case of noncom pliance on the part of Prussia Russian troops were to invade Prussian Silesia and France was to oc cupv the possessions of Prussia on the Rhine England 1 aid not to have acceded to this propo sition and to have advised a separate remonstrance of each of the three Powers against the faithless

results.title: Image 2 of Daily national intelligencer (Washington City [D.C.]), March 23, 1863
description: INTELLIGENCER FROM PORT ROYAL We have intelligence from Port Royal to the 16th inst No change of importance is reported in military or naval affairs It is said that large reinforcements supposed to be Burnsides army corps were expected at Hilton Head The story heretofore received of the capture of the ne gro expedition which was lately sent to Florida under the command of Col Higginson and Col Montgomery is un true The expedition after completing all necessary pre parations for the inland march at Fernandina moved up St Johns liver and on the 10th instant occupied the town of Jacksonville without meeting any resistance There was a small body of rebel troops in town at the time of the appearance of the black troops but they aban doned it hastily and retreated towards the interior The Port Royal New South of the 14th instant contains the following paragraph on the subject By the arrival of the transport Boston this morning we learn that tlie city of Jacksonville was taken by the First South Carolina Volunteers under Col Higgiuson on the 10th instant The people were in great fear as the black troops marched up the streets of an indiscrimi

description: WEEKLY JOURNAL JOHN MAHIN EDITOR CITY OF IHVSCAT1NE fRIDAY JULY 5 1861 REPUBLICAN STATE CONVENTION A StAt Convention of the Republican of Iowa is tailed to assemble by their delegate at the City ot Moines at 10 oclock A U on Wednesday July 31st 1801 for the nomination of officers to be elected at tht genoral election in October of this year Cnudidatee re to selected for the offices of Governor Lieutenant governor and one Judge of the Supreme Court The Convention will take such otlur action as may in iU Opinion contribute to the ucces of the Republican Srery organized county will be entitled to one dele gat in the Convention and one additional delegate fur each 900 votes and for any additional fraction of or more votes cast at the last General Election tor Uon Abraham Lincoln as President vt thr I nited State Republican County Central Committee are requested to call their conventions to select their delegates in the ratio aboe stated and for efficient organisation prepar atory to the canvass H M HOXIE Chairman State Central Com Samuel F Miller Lee County J B Weaver Davis George A llawley Pecatur Levi it Smith Koenuth W B
